rules.title: Slovak Cases - Complete Overview
rules.text: Slovak has 7 grammatical cases that indicate the function of nouns in sentences. Each case answers specific questions and is triggered by certain verbs, prepositions, or grammatical contexts.

rows: bez, do, od, z, u, okolo, počas
na, za, cez, pre, o, v (motion)
s/so, medzi, pred, nad, pod, za

examples: Nominative: Muž pracuje – The man works
Genitive: Nemám psa – I don't have a dog
Dative: Pomáham priateľovi – I help a friend
Accusative: Vidím dom – I see a house
Locative: Som v meste – I'm in the city
Instrumental: Píšem perom – I write with a pen

rules.title: Nominative Case - Subject and Identity
rules.text: The nominative is the basic dictionary form. Used for: 1) Subject of sentence, 2) Predicate nominative (after 'byť' = to be), 3) Direct address/vocative function.
examples: Subject: Študent číta knihu – The student reads a book
Predicate: Ja som učiteľ – I am a teacher
Predicate: To je moja sestra – That is my sister
Address: Dobrý deň, pán Novák! – Good day, Mr. Novák!
Multiple subjects: Otec a matka pracujú – Father and mother work

rules.title: Genitive Case - Possession
rules.text: The genitive expresses possession (whose?), similar to English 's or 'of'. Word order: possessed + possessor.
examples: Dom otca – Father's house (the house of father)
Kniha študenta – The student's book
Koniec ulice – The end of the street
Farba auta – The color of the car
Dcéra učiteľa – The teacher's daughter
Dvere domu – The doors of the house

rules.title: Genitive Case - Negation
rules.text: After negated verbs, the direct object changes from accusative to genitive. This is a crucial rule in Slovak.
examples: Positive: Mám psa (A) – I have a dog
Negative: Nemám psa (G) – I don't have a dog
Positive: Vidím auto (A) – I see a car
Negative: Nevidím auta (G) – I don't see a car
Positive: Poznám túto ženu (A) – I know this woman
Negative: Nepoznám túto ženu (G) – I don't know this woman

rules.title: Genitive Case - Quantity and Numbers
rules.text: The genitive is used after numbers 5 and above, and after quantity expressions.
examples: päť mužov – five men (genitive plural)
desať žien – ten women (genitive plural)
dvadsať rokov – twenty years
veľa peňazí – a lot of money (genitive)
málo času – little time (genitive)
dosť vody – enough water (genitive)
koľko ľudí? – how many people? (genitive)

rules.title: Genitive Prepositions
rules.text: Common prepositions that require genitive case.
examples: Idem do obchodu – I'm going to the store
Prichádzam od doktora – I'm coming from the doctor
Bez teba – Without you
Okolo domu – Around the house

rules.title: Dative Case - Indirect Object
rules.text: The dative indicates the indirect object (to whom/for whom something is given or done). Common with verbs of giving, helping, saying.
examples: Dávam knihu priateľovi – I give a book to a friend
Pomáham matke – I help mother
Hovorím učiteľovi – I'm speaking to the teacher
Kupujem darček sestre – I'm buying a gift for sister
Píšem list otcovi – I'm writing a letter to father
Volám bratovi – I'm calling brother

rules.title: Verbs Requiring Dative
rules.text: Common verbs that take dative case for their object.
examples: Pomôžem ti – I'll help you
Verím priateľovi – I believe my friend
Nerozumiem mu – I don't understand him
Ďakujem vám – Thank you (formal)

rules.title: Dative Prepositions
rules.text: Prepositions that require dative case.
examples: Idem k priateľovi – I'm going to a friend's
Vďaka učiteľovi – Thanks to the teacher
Kvôli dažďu – Because of the rain
Proti vetru – Against the wind

rules.title: Accusative Case - Direct Object
rules.text: The accusative marks the direct object (whom/what the action directly affects). This is the most common case after nominative.
examples: Vidím muža – I see a man
Čítam knihu – I'm reading a book
Kupujem auto – I'm buying a car
Poznám učiteľa – I know the teacher
Mám psa – I have a dog
Volám sestru – I'm calling sister

rules.title: Accusative with Motion Prepositions
rules.text: Accusative is used with prepositions indicating motion or direction (where to?).

rows: I'm going to the post office

examples: Idem na trh – I'm going to the market
Dávam knihu na stôl – I put the book on the table
Cez most – Across the bridge
Pre mňa – For me

rules.title: Accusative - Time Expressions
rules.text: Accusative is used for time expressions answering 'when?' or 'for how long?'
examples: Idem v pondelok – I'm going on Monday
Celý deň – All day (accusative)
Budúci týždeň – Next week
Minulý rok – Last year
Každú stredu – Every Wednesday
Túto sobotu – This Saturday
Celú noc – All night

rules.title: Locative Case - Location (Static)
rules.text: Locative indicates static location (where? - no motion) and is ALWAYS used with a preposition. Never appears without a preposition.
examples: Som v meste – I'm in the city (static)
Kniha je na stole – The book is on the table
Bývam v Bratislave – I live in Bratislava
Sedím pri okne – I'm sitting by the window
Pracujem v škole – I work at school
Je to v krabici – It's in the box

rules.title: Locative Prepositions - Location
rules.text: Common prepositions requiring locative for static location.
examples: V škole – In school
Na ulici – On the street
Pri rieke – By the river
Po večeri – After dinner
Hovorím o filme – I'm talking about a movie

rules.title: Locative vs Accusative - Motion vs Location
rules.text: Key distinction: Accusative = motion/direction (where to?), Locative = static location (where?).

rows: I'm going to the post office
I put the book on the table
The book is on the table

examples: Idem na trh (A) – I'm going to the market (motion)
Som na trhu (L) – I'm at the market (location)
Idem do obchodu (G with 'do') – I'm going to the store
Som v obchode (L) – I'm in the store

rules.title: Instrumental Case - Means and Tools
rules.text: Instrumental expresses the means or tool by which an action is performed (with what?).
examples: Píšem perom – I write with a pen
Režem nožom – I cut with a knife
Idem autobusom – I go by bus
Platím kartou – I pay with a card
Otváram kľúčom – I open with a key
Pracujem počítačom – I work with a computer

rules.title: Instrumental Case - Accompaniment
rules.text: Instrumental expresses accompaniment (with whom?).
examples: Idem s priateľom – I'm going with a friend
Hovorím so sestrou – I'm talking with sister
Stretávam sa s učiteľom – I'm meeting with the teacher
Pracujem s kolegom – I work with a colleague
Bývam s rodičmi – I live with parents
Tancujem s ňou – I dance with her

rules.title: Instrumental Case - Professions and States
rules.text: Instrumental is used after 'byť' (to be) for professions, roles, and temporary states.
examples: Som učiteľom – I am a teacher
Je lekárom – He is a doctor
Bola študentkou – She was a student
Stane sa prezidentom – He will become president
Je mojím priateľom – He is my friend
Bol dobrým človekom – He was a good person

rules.title: Instrumental Prepositions
rules.text: Common prepositions requiring instrumental case.
examples: Idem s bratom – I'm going with brother
Medzi nami – Between us
Pred domom – In front of the house
Nad hlavou – Above the head
Pod mostom – Under the bridge

rules.title: Prepositions with Multiple Cases
rules.text: Some prepositions can take different cases depending on meaning (motion vs. location).
examples: na + A: Idem na trh – I'm going to the market
na + L: Som na trhu – I'm at the market
za + A: Idem za dom – I'm going behind the house
za + I: Som za domom – I'm behind the house

rules.title: Case Selection Quick Guide
rules.text: Decision tree for choosing the correct case.

rows: After bez, do, od, z, u?
